--- title: "Obtaining, manipulating, and fitting climate data" subtitle: "SPARKLE Session 2 on climate data in infectious disease modelling" format: training-html: default presenter: "Michael & Roslyn" contributors: - "Dr Vassili Kitsios" - "Prof. Roslyn Hickson" - "Dr Michael Lydeamore" - "Claude (Anthropic) did the translation of the existing Python workflow to R Quarto; all content to be checked by the course team" --- ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: ::: speaker-note **Environment.** The R practicals assume the following packages are installed: `jsonlite`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `lubridate`, `ggplot2`, `patchwork`, `sf`, `terra`, and `ncdf4`. Please confirm these are installed before the session. The geospatial steps (reanalysis and CMIP6) read netCDF and shapefiles directly and can take a little while on first render. Using `freeze: auto` caches them after the first successful render. **Worked example.** Throughout, the running example is malaria incidence in Thailand, linked to ERA5 reanalysis climate and CMIP6 future projections. ::: # Summary {.tabset .tabset-pills .tabset-fade} This session works through the practical workflow of using climate data in infectious disease modelling, with malaria in Thailand as the case study: from obtaining raw data through to a fitted statistical model that links climate to malaria incidence, with future projections. The session opens by framing the **research question** and then works through six practical parts, with an extension section on fitting an SEIRS transmission model: **The research question.** What we are trying to answer, and how it informs what data and modelling we need. **Part 1: Obtaining the data.** Where the data come from (malaria surveillance, climate reanalysis, future projections, administrative boundaries) and how to obtain them. **Part 2: Malaria case data.** Reading the surveillance line list and summarising cases by month, province, and district. **Part 3: Environmental variables (reanalysis).** Linking gridded ERA5 climate data to administrative units using a shapefile, and building per-province monthly temperature and rainfall series. **Part 4: Future climate projections.** Summarising CMIP6 projections under two emissions scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5) for the same provinces. **Part 5: Fitting climate to incidence.** A statistical (ARIMA/ARX panel) model relating standardised climate to changes in malaria cases across provinces. **Part 6: Projecting future cases.** Pushing the fitted statistical model forward under the CMIP6 scenarios to project future cases, with the important caveats of long-range extrapolation. **Optional extension: An SEIRS model.** A (basic) transmission model approach. ::: {.callout-tip} We recommend saving your written answers at the end of each part by printing the page (there is a **Print this page** button at the end of each part). ::: This is a port of the python analysis (`python_workflow/malaria_workflow.ipynb`), which we have left available for those interested. --- # The research question ::: speaker-note Spend the first few minutes establishing *what we are trying to answer* and *what decision it would inform*. Draw out that the question is what tells us (a) whether climate data is even relevant, (b) which data we need, and (c) whether it makes sense to project forward. Participants do this same first step for their own work in Session 3. ::: It is important in modelling for the question to determine the methodology, and it also informs what data we need. The data available may in turn force us to update and refine our question and methodology. For this case study, our research question is: > **How do temperature and rainfall relate to malaria incidence across Thailand, > and how might that incidence change under future emissions scenarios?** Reading that question tells us: - a measure of the **outcome**: malaria cases, over time and by location. - the **drivers** we hypothesise to matter: temperature and rainfall. - a **spatial region**: Thailand. The spatial resolution is often driven by data availability. In this case, we will use provinces (the unit cases are reported in). - a **historical period** where we observe outcome and drivers together, to \ *learn* the relationship. This is also often driven by data availability. - **future climate** for the same region, to *project* that relationship forward. This list is the data we found available, not the initial list assembled. **What data do you think is missing from this list, and why?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Suggested answer: click to reveal (try to answer yourself first) There is no single right answer. We were initially planning on relating mosquito vector abundance to the climatic variables, and projecting those forward and linking to malaria incidence via a transmission model tracking humans and vectors. But we couldn't find abundance data in Thailand at the same time period as the malaria incidence data. ::: --- # **Part 1:** Obtaining the data ::: speaker-note This part is about *where the data come from* and *why each source was chosen*. ::: The workflow draws on four data sources. Three are used directly in the analysis; the fourth (mosquito occurrence) motivates the approach but is not currently used. The table below is the quick reference; each source, and why it was chosen, is expanded underneath. | # | Data | What it is / role | Why this source | Source | |---|--------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------| | 1 | Malaria cases | national surveillance line list (the outcome) | authoritative, national, open, right resolution | [Thai DDC](https://malaria-api.ddc-malaria.org/study/2year/calendar){target="_blank"} | | 2 | Spatial region boundaries | province (ADM1) polygons; aggregate grid to province | official ADM1; names align with the case data | [Open Dev Mekong](https://data.opendevelopmentmekong.net/dataset/thailand-provincial-boundaries){target="_blank"} | | 3 | ERA5 reanalysis | gap-free past climate, used to *learn* | state of the art, ~0.25°, complete coverage, open | [Copernicus CDS](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels?tab=download){target="_blank"} | | 4 | CMIP6 projections | multi-model future climate, used to *project* | latest coordinated ensemble; scenarios bracket futures | [Copernicus CDS](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/projections-cmip6?tab=download){target="_blank"} | ::: {.callout-tip} Reanalysis (ERA5) is used to *learn* the relationship between climate and malaria over the historical period; the CMIP6 projections are used to *project* that relationship forward under future emissions scenarios. ::: **1. Malaria case data (surveillance line list).** Individual records of malaria blood draws in Thailand, with date, province, district, and result (*P. vivax* `V`, *P. falciparum* `F`, or other). This is the outcome we are modelling, and it comes from the national surveillance system (Thailand's DDC), so it is authoritative and consistent across the whole country. It is openly queryable via the API (no data-sharing agreement for this aggregated endpoint) and carries date, province, district and species per record, at the spatial and temporal resolution we need for monthly climate. Alternatives like WHO country-year totals are too coarse, and hospital records are not openly accessible. *Source and files:* [https://malaria-api.ddc-malaria.org/study/2year/calendar](https://malaria-api.ddc-malaria.org/study/2year/calendar){target="_blank"} , saved as `data/malaria/calendar.json`. ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} **DDC** is Thailand's Department of Disease Control (its vector-borne disease division runs the malaria surveillance). ::: **2. Administrative boundaries (shape files).** Province boundaries for Thailand. We need province polygons because cases are reported per province but climate is gridded; the polygons let us aggregate the grid to the reporting unit. This set was chosen because it is the official Thai ADM1 (province) boundaries, so its units and names align with how the surveillance data is reported (name alignment matters: it is why some provinces drop out when names do not match), and it is freely available in a standard GIS format. *Source and files:* [https://data.opendevelopmentmekong.net/dataset/thailand-provincial-boundaries](https://data.opendevelopmentmekong.net/dataset/thailand-provincial-boundaries){target="_blank"} , saved under `data/shape_files/tha_admbnda_adm1_rtsd_20190221/`. ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} A **shape file** stores polygon boundaries (countries, provinces, districts); **ADM1** is the first sub-national administrative level, here the provinces. ::: **3. Climate reanalysis (ERA5).** Gridded, monthly-averaged 2 m temperature (`2t`) and total precipitation (`tp`): a physically consistent, gap-free reconstruction of past weather, used here to *learn* how climate relates to malaria. We use reanalysis rather than weather stations because stations are sparse and gappy, whereas reanalysis gives complete fields everywhere, which is essential for giving every province a climate value. We use ERA5 specifically because it is the current state-of-the-art global reanalysis (~0.25° resolution, well validated, openly available), carrying the variables we want (temperature and precipitation drive mosquito abundance and survival) at monthly resolution. It is the de facto standard for climate and health work. *Source and files:* [https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels?tab=download](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels?tab=download){target="_blank"} , saved under `data/reanalysis/monthly-averaged/`. The required subset is already curated for you; other variables can be downloaded freely from the Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) Climate Data Store. ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} **ERA5** = ECMWF Re-Analysis, 5th generation (ECMWF is the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts; the lineage runs ERA-15, ERA-40, ERA-Interim, ERA5). ::: **4. Future climate projections (CMIP6).** A multi-model ensemble of monthly temperature (`tas`) and precipitation (`pr`) for Thailand under two emissions scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5), used to *project* the learned relationship into the future. We use CMIP6 specifically because it is the latest coordinated multi-model generation (the models underpinning IPCC AR6), delivered in a standard, comparable format, so we can use a multi-model ensemble to represent model uncertainty and the SSP scenarios to bracket futures. SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5 were chosen as a low- and high-emissions pair to show sensitivity to the pathway. It is also regriddable onto the ERA5 grid, so the historical and future steps stay consistent. *Source and files:* [https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/projections-cmip6?tab=download](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/projections-cmip6?tab=download){target="_blank"} , saved under `data/cmip6/monthly-averaged/`. The data is curated and trimmed to the region for this case study; other variables, scenarios, or the full globe are available from the same Climate Data Store. ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} **CMIP6** = Coupled Model Intercomparison Project, 6th phase ("coupled" refers to coupled atmosphere and ocean models). In an **SSP** (Shared Socioeconomic Pathway) label such as SSP1-2.6, the first digit is the socioeconomic story (1 = sustainability, 5 = fossil-fuel-intensive) and the second is the resulting radiative forcing (2.6 vs 8.5 W/m² by 2100, i.e. low vs high warming). ::: **Mosquito occurrence (GBIF), considered but not used.** Mosquito occurrence records for Thailand. We explored GBIF because it is the largest aggregator of open occurrence records, so it is the obvious first place to look for vector data. It is not used here because it gives *occurrence*, not *abundance*, and has no samples after 2022, so it does not overlap the 2025 to 2026 malaria data. For now we relate malaria incidence in humans to environmental variables directly (the approach taken in Part 5); regardless, this is a good, straightforward way to test whether environmental variables such as these are affecting the health outcomes of interest, and which ones. A natural extension is to obtain overlapping mosquito data, learn the influence of temperature on the mosquito population, then the impact of that population on malaria infections via a transmission model. *Source and files:* [https://www.gbif.org/dataset/db4211d6-b480-4bc5-9202-87f2072c4b12](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/db4211d6-b480-4bc5-9202-87f2072c4b12){target="_blank"} (not downloaded; not part of the analysis). ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} **GBIF** is the Global Biodiversity Information Facility, the largest aggregator of open species-occurrence records. ::: ::: {.callout-note} ## Obtaining the data yourself The Copernicus Climate Data Store (ERA5 and CMIP6) requires a free account and, for bulk downloads, the CDS API (a `~/.cdsapirc` key file). Because these downloads need accounts/API keys and can be large, they are not run as code here. Instead, the curated subset needed for this tutorial is provided separately in the `data/` folder at the repository root. ::: ## Ideal vs achievable workflows ::: speaker-note The key discussion point here: what you *want* to do is often constrained by the data you can actually get. ::: The right workflow is rarely the most sophisticated one, but one that fits both your question and that your data can actually support. For malaria, the biologically known climate-sensitive link is the *mosquito* population: temperature and breeding site available, typically via rainfall, drive mosquito abundance, which in turn drives transmission. | Approach | Pipeline | Data needed | Biological fidelity | |---|---|---|---| | **Ideal** (vector-explicit) | climate → mosquito abundance → transmission model → incidence | overlapping spatio-temporal local mosquito **abundance** data *and* climate | highest | | **Simplest** (direct statistical) | climate → incidence | overlapping spatio-temporal case data + climate | lowest: no explicit mechanism | | **Achievable here** (climate-forced SEIRS) | climate → transmission model (transmission forced by climate) → incidence | overlapping spatio-temporal case data + climate | medium: climate link through implicit vector dynamics | For this example we wanted the **ideal** path, but we couldn't find mosquito abundance data that spatio-temporally overlapped the malaria period (see the GBIF note above), so we use the lower two tiers: a direct statistical model (Part 5) and a climate-forced SEIRS model (the optional section at the end of this session). Both skip the mosquito layer. This is a data-driven compromise. **1.1 For each of the four data sources above, what alternative dataset or approach could you use in its place? Think about substitutes for ERA5, for CMIP6, and for the provincial case and boundary data, and what you would trade off by switching. If you identfied other data earlier, what sources are there?** ```{=html} ```


**1.2 Reanalysis is used to *learn* the climate–disease relationship and CMIP6 projections are used to *project* it into the future. Why can we not simply use the future projections to learn the relationship in the first place?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 1: click to reveal (try the questions first) **1.1** There are alternatives for every source, each with trade-offs. For the reanalysis, other products such as MERRA-2 or JRA-55, or gridded observational datasets (for example CHIRPS for rainfall, CRU for temperature), could replace ERA5, trading spatial and temporal resolution and which variables are available. For the projections, CMIP5 or a downscaled or emulated ensemble could replace CMIP6, changing resolution, the scenario definitions, and the model spread. For the boundaries, a different administrative product or a gridded population layer could be used, changing which spatial unit cases are aggregated to. For the case data, a different health outcome or a modelled incidence surface could stand in where line-list surveillance is not available. The point is to notice that each swap changes resolution, coverage, or assumptions rather than to find one right answer. **1.2** Future projections are, by construction, *projections*: they describe plausible futures, not observed weather, and there is no malaria data from the future to pair them with. We can only learn a relationship where we observe *both* climate and cases together, which means the historical/reanalysis period. The projections are then used to *project* that learned relationship forward. ::: --- # **Part 2:** Malaria case data ::: speaker-note This part reads the surveillance line list and aggregates it. The key teaching points are: (1) surveillance data are messy and unevenly reported across provinces and time; this is why we later restrict to provinces with enough data; and (2) aggregating to monthly counts per province gives us the response variable that we will link to climate. The code is a direct translation of the Python `pandas` workflow. Have participants run it and *consider* the province panels (question 2.2) before moving on. ::: We start from the surveillance line list: one row per blood draw, with a date, province, district, and result. We read it, parse the dates, and aggregate to monthly counts. **Run the code below, then work through the questions underneath it.** First read in the data from the json file. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(jsonlite) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(lubridate) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork) library(here) # Locate the data data_dir <- here::here("data") # Read the JSON line list and parse dates --------------------------------- df <- fromJSON(file.path(data_dir, "malaria", "calendar.json")) df <- df %>% mutate( date_daily = as.Date(as.character(blood_draw_date), format = "%Y%m%d"), month = floor_date(date_daily, "month") ) # Preview what we have loaded --------------------------------------------- knitr::kable(head(df, 5)) # the first 5 rows as a clean table in quarto cat("Number of unique province names:", n_distinct(df$province_en), "\n") cat("Number of unique district names:", n_distinct(df$district_en), "\n") cat("Number of unique blood_draw_date:", n_distinct(df$blood_draw_date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} |data_guid |blood_draw_date |sex |province_id |province_th |province_en |district_id ``` ::: ::: Counts for Thailand per month and per day ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Total counts per day and per month -------------------------------------- counts_per_day <- df %>% count(date_daily, name = "count") %>% arrange(date_daily) counts_per_month <- df %>% count(month, name = "count") %>% arrange(month) p_day <- ggplot(counts_per_day, aes(date_daily, count)) + geom_line() + scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 month", date_labels = "%b %Y") + labs(x = "date", y = "total daily counts") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) p_month <- ggplot(counts_per_month, aes(month, count)) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 1) + scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 month", date_labels = "%b %Y") + labs(x = "date (monthly total, aligned to first of per province per month, including the impact of the climate. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Counts per province per month ------------------------------------------- counts_prov_month <- df %>% count(month, province_en, name = "Count") %>% arrange(month, province_en) # Keep provinces with more than 12 months of reported cases prov_order <- unique(counts_prov_month$province_en) selected_provinces <- prov_order[ vapply(prov_order, function(p) sum(counts_prov_month$province_en == p) > 12, logical(1)) ] cat("Selected provinces:", length(selected_provinces), "\n") # Facet titles: how many of the possible months each province reports --------- n_possible <- n_distinct(counts_prov_month$month) # total months in the data (18) prov_counts <- counts_prov_month %>% filter(province_en %in% selected_provinces) %>% group_by(province_en) %>% summarise(n_months = n_distinct(month), .groups = "drop") prov_lab <- setNames( paste0(prov_counts$province_en, " (", prov_counts$n_months, "/", n_possible, ")"), prov_counts$province_en ) print(counts_prov_month %>% filter(province_en %in% selected_provinces) %>% ggplot(aes(month, Count)) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~ province_en, scales = "free_y", labeller = labeller(province_en = prov_lab)) + labs(x = "date", y = "monthly count") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part2-province-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} n_months_total <- n_distinct(df$month) # 18 possible months dist_months <- counts_dist_month %>% group_by(district_en) %>% summarise(n_months = n_distinct(month), .groups = "drop") cat("Districts in reported cases: 37 Provinces with >12 months (from above): 18 The 37 qualifying districts hold 90.8% of all reported cases The 18 qualifying provinces hold 97.5% of all reported cases ``` ::: ::: **2.1 How many provinces are reported in the surveillance data, and how many are kept after the "more than 12 months" filter? Why might we want to restrict to provinces with a longer record before trying to relate cases to climate?** ```{=html} ```


**2.2 Why analyse at the province scale? What do we gain and lose by treating the province as the spatial unit, and what would push you to a finer or coarser scale?** ```{=html} ```


**2.3 Look at the per-province panels. Describe the seasonal pattern you see. Do all provinces peak at the same time of year? What might explain differences between provinces?** ```{=html} ```


**2.4 The results column pools *P. vivax* and *P. falciparum* together. Why might it matter, for a climate-driven analysis, that these two species are combined?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 2: click to reveal (try the questions first) **2.1** There are many provinces in the raw data, but only those reported in more than 12 months are kept (18 provinces kept out of 66), and of those only the ones whose names match the boundary shapefile are carried forward. Restricting to provinces with a longer record means each province has enough months to show a seasonal signal and to standardise sensibly; provinces with only a handful of reported months cannot support a climate–seasonality analysis. **2.2** The spatial unit is a trade-off between signal and sparsity, and the case counts sharpen it. Going finer would likely work, with 90.8% of the malaria data still included. But visualising the 37 districts that clear the ">12 months" rule vs the 18 provinces is more difficult. It would also spread almost the same case burden across roughly twice as many units (37 districts versus 18 provinces), so each unit's monthly counts are smaller and noisier, and 303 of the 340 districts are too sparse to use at all. The province scale captures nearly all cases (97.5%) in the fewest robust units, and so it is a pragmatic choice. Going finer would suit a focused analysis of the high-burden districts; going coarser (national) would give complete series but average over very d ifferent climates and malaria regimes,likely washing out the signal. **2.3** Most provinces show a seasonal rise and fall, but not all peak in the same month; malaria seasonality depends on local climate, elevation, land use, and the timing of the rains, which differ across the country. Border provinces and forested provinces often behave differently from central ones. **2.4** *P. vivax* and *P. falciparum* differ in incubation period, in relapse behaviour (vivax can relapse from liver-stage hypnozoites months later), and likely in their climate sensitivity. But they have similar mosquito vectors. So whether or not pooling them means blurring the climate-malaria signal depends on how climate-related those factors are, and whether the mosquito relationship with climate would dominate. A potential future direction to explore. ::: --- # **Part 3:** Environmental variables (reanalysis) ::: speaker-note This part links gridded climate to the administrative units that malaria is reported in. The conceptual step to draw out is the *spatial mismatch*: climate comes on a regular latitude–longitude grid, but cases come per province. We resolve it in two steps. First, **strict cell-centre assignment**: a grid cell belongs to the province whose polygon contains the cell's *centre* (a point-in-polygon test, matching the `regionmask` package). Second, the province's cells are combined with an **area-weighted average**, each cell weighted by cos of its latitude because grid cells shrink towards the poles; this is the standard spatial mean (see the aside on population weighting). Participants often ask why we keep temperature in Kelvin during standardisation: because a constant offset (Kelvin vs Celsius) does not change a standardised (z-scored) series, so it only matters for the plots, where we convert to °C. ::: Malaria is reported per province, but ERA5 climate comes on a regular grid. To link them, we summarise the grid to provinces in two steps: **strict cell-centre assignment**, where each grid cell is assigned to the province whose polygon contains the cell's centre (a point-in-polygon test with `sf`, matching the [`regionmask`](https://regionmask.readthedocs.io){target="_blank"} package), and then an **area-weighted average** of those cells, weighting each by cos of its latitude so that cells count in proportion to their area. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Why cos of latitude is the exact area weight On a regular grid every cell spans the same number of degrees in latitude and longitude, but equal degrees are not equal area: meridians converge towards the poles, so a cell's east-west width shrinks with latitude while its north-south height stays fixed. The area of the cell between latitudes `lat₁` and `lat₂`, over a longitude span `Δλ` on a sphere of radius `R`, is `A = R² · Δλ · (sin(lat₂) - sin(lat₁))`. For a cell of height `Δlat` centred at latitude `lat`, the identity `sin(lat + Δlat/2) - sin(lat - Δlat/2) = 2 · cos(lat) · sin(Δlat/2)` turns this into `A = R² · Δλ · 2 · sin(Δlat/2) · cos(lat)`. Every factor except `cos(lat)` is identical for all cells on a regular grid, and constant factors cancel in a normalised weighted mean, so weighting each cell by cos of its centre latitude reproduces the true area weighting exactly. It is not a small-angle approximation: it is exact for a sphere. The Earth's slight oblateness shifts cell areas by a fraction of a percent, far below anything that matters at this provincial scale. ::: ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} We weight each cell by its area (cos of latitude), the standard spatial mean. For climate and health work an even better choice is **population-density weighting**, which captures the climate where people actually live. That matters most for countries with very uneven populations (for example Australia, where most people are concentrated in a few coastal cities); at Thailand's provincial scale the difference is small, so we keep area-weighting here. ::: **Run the code below, then work through the questions.** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(sf) library(terra) # Province (ADM1) boundaries ---------------------------------------------- shape_file <- st_read( file.path(data_dir, "shape_files", "tha_admbnda_adm1_rtsd_20190221", "tha_admbnda_adm1_rtsd_20190221.shp"), quiet = TRUE ) region_names <- shape_file$ADM1_EN cat("Number of provinces in shapefile:", length(region_names), "\n") # The surveillance province names must match the shapefile's ADM1_EN names to be # mapped. Keep only the selected provinces that are present in the boundaries # (mirrors the original workflow, which reduces the province list to those found # in the shapefile). Report any that are dropped so name mismatches are visible. missing <- setdiff(selected_provinces, region_names) if (length(missing)) message("Provinces not matched in shapefile and dropped: ", paste(missing, collapse = ", ")) selected_provinces <- selected_provinces[selected_provinces %in% region_names] cat("Provinces carried forward (matched to boundaries):", length(selected_provinces), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Number of provinces in shapefile: 77 Provinces carried forward (matched to boundaries): 15 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # ERA5 monthly reanalysis: 2 m temperature (2t -> t2m) and precip (tp) ----- # One netCDF file per month; sorting the file paths puts them in time order. tas_files <- sort(list.files( file.path(data_dir, "reanalysis", "monthly-averaged", "2t"), pattern = "\\.nc$", recursive = TRUE, full.names = TRUE)) pr_files <- sort(list.files( file.path(data_dir, "reanalysis", "monthly-averaged", "tp"), pattern = "\\.nc$", recursive = TRUE, full.names = TRUE)) tas_r <- rast(tas_files) # temperature, one layer per month (Kelvin) pr_r <- rast(pr_files) # total precipitation, one layer per month (metres) # Restrict to the Thailand region used in the original workflow region_ext <- ext(97, 108, 5, 22) tas_r <- crop(tas_r, region_ext) pr_r <- crop(pr_r, region_ext) # Recover the month for each layer from the filename (YYYYMMDD-...) month_from_file <- function(f) floor_date(as.Date(sub(".*_(\\d{8})-\\d{8}\\.nc$", "\\1", basename(f)), format = "%Y%m%d"), "month") clim_months <- month_from_file(tas_files) cat("Reanalysis covers", length(clim_months), "months:", format(min(clim_months)), "to", format(max(clim_months)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Reanalysis covers 15 months: 2025-01-01 to 2026-03-01 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Map of the time-mean temperature and rainfall over the region ----------- tas_mean_c <- mean(tas_r) - 273.15 # Kelvin -> Celsius for display pr_mean <- mean(pr_r) map_df_t <- as.data.frame(tas_mean_c, xy = TRUE); names(map_df_t)[3] <- "value" map_df_r <- as.data.frame(pr_mean, xy = TRUE); names(map_df_r)[3] <- "value" m_t <- ggplot(map_df_t, aes(x, y, fill = value)) + geom_raster() + geom_sf(data = shape_file, fill = NA, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.2, inherit.aes = FALSE) + scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "°C") + coord_sf(expand = FALSE) + labs(title = "Mean temperature", x = "lon", y = "lat") + theme_bw() m_r <- ggplot(map_df_r, aes(x, y, fill = value)) + geom_raster() + geom_sf(data = shape_file, fill = NA, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.2, inherit.aes = FALSE) + scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "m", option = "mako") + coord_sf(expand = FALSE) + labs(title = "Mean rainfall", x = "lon", y = "lat") + theme_bw() m_t + m_r ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part3-maps-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Assign each grid cell to a province by STRICT CELL-CENTRE assignment -------- # A cell belongs to the province whose polygon contains the cell's *centre*, and # the provincial value is the simple mean over those cells. This reproduces the # standard Python geospatial workflow used in the original analysis (the # `regionmask` package), rather than relying on the raster library's own polygon # extraction, whose boundary handling can differ slightly. grid_xy <- terra::xyFromCell(tas_r, seq_len(terra::ncell(tas_r))) grid_pts <- sf::st_as_sf(as.data.frame(grid_xy), coords = c("x", "y"), crs = sf::st_crs(shape_file)) within <- sf::st_within(grid_pts, shape_file) # cell centre in polygon? cell_region <- vapply(within, function(h) if (length(h)) h[1] else NA_integer_, integer(1)) # Provincial mean for every layer (time step), area-weighted by cell size. Grid # cells shrink towards the poles, so each cell is weighted by cos(latitude); this is # the standard, physically correct spatial average. The cell assignment and weights # are shared by the reanalysis and (after resampling) the CMIP data, so they are # reused in Part 4. cell_w <- cos(grid_xy[, 2] * pi / 180) # per-cell area weight (cos latitude) region_means <- function(r) { vals <- terra::values(r) # ncell x nlayer out <- matrix(NA_real_, length(region_names), ncol(vals), dimnames = list(region_names, NULL)) for (k in seq_along(region_names)) { sel <- which(cell_region == k) if (!length(sel)) next w <- cell_w[sel] out[k, ] <- colSums(vals[sel, , drop = FALSE] * w, na.rm = TRUE) / sum(w) } out } tas_prov <- region_means(tas_r) pr_prov <- region_means(pr_r) # Tidy into long tables, keeping only the selected provinces to_long <- function(mat, value_name) { m <- as.matrix(mat) rownames(m) <- region_names colnames(m) <- as.character(clim_months) as.data.frame(m) %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("province_en") %>% pivot_longer(-province_en, names_to = "month", values_to = value_name) %>% mutate(month = as.Date(month)) } clim_long <- to_long(tas_prov, "temp_k") %>% left_join(to_long(pr_prov, "rain_m"), by = c("province_en", "month")) %>% mutate(temp_c = temp_k - 273.15) %>% filter(province_en %in% selected_provinces) print(head(clim_long)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 5 province_en month temp_k rain_m temp_c 1 Bangkok 2025-01-01 298. 0.00000870 24.8 2 Bangkok 2025-02-01 301. 0.00134 27.8 3 Bangkok 2025-03-01 303. 0.00123 29.5 4 Bangkok 2025-04-01 304. 0.00251 30.6 5 Bangkok 2025-05-01 303. 0.00728 29.6 6 Bangkok 2025-06-01 303. 0.00336 30.0 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Per-province monthly temperature and rainfall series -------------------- print(ggplot(clim_long, aes(month, temp_c)) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 0.5) + facet_wrap(~ province_en, scales = "free_y") + labs(x = "date", y = "monthly mean temperature (°C)") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part3-series-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: **3.1 We averaged climate over each province. What information do we lose by doing this? For a large province with varied terrain, is a single provincial average a good summary of the climate that mosquitoes and people experience?** ```{=html} ```


**3.2 Look at the per-province temperature series. How large is the within-year temperature range compared to the differences between provinces? Which do you think will be more useful for explaining malaria seasonality?** ```{=html} ```


**3.3 The reanalysis only covers a couple of years here. What are the risks of learning a climate–malaria relationship from such a short record?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 3: click to reveal (try the questions first) **3.1** Averaging to a province discards all within-province spatial variation: elevation gradients, forest edges, urban vs rural. For a large or topographically varied province, a single average may not represent the conditions where transmission actually happens (often specific ecological niches), so the link to cases is weakened. **3.2** Typically the within-year (seasonal) temperature swing within a province is modest compared with, say, rainfall seasonality, and provinces differ from each other too. Both the seasonal cycle and the between-province contrasts carry information; the panel model in Part 5 uses both by standardising within province and pooling across provinces. **3.3** A short record risks fitting to year-specific anomalies (an unusual season, a one-off outbreak) rather than a stable climate–malaria relationship, and gives very little power to separate climate effects from other drivers. Projections built on such a fit should be treated as illustrative. ::: --- # **Part 4:** Future climate projections (CMIP6) ::: speaker-note This part summarises the CMIP6 multi-model ensemble to the same provinces, under two scenarios. The key ideas for participants: (1) CMIP6 is an *ensemble* of many climate models; we summarise across models with a mean and an interquartile band, rather than trusting any single model; and (2) the two scenarios (SSP1-2.6 low emissions, SSP5-8.5 high emissions) bracket a range of plausible futures. This is a direct translation of the original Python, including the precipitation unit conversion. It is the heaviest step in the document; on a slow machine give it a minute, and note that `freeze: auto` caches it afterwards. ::: The learned relationship (Part 5) is only useful if we can apply it to the future. CMIP6 provides projections of temperature (`tas`) and precipitation (`pr`) from many climate models, under different emissions scenarios. We summarise them to the same provinces, under two scenarios: **SSP1-2.6** (low emissions) and **SSP5-8.5** (high emissions). **Run the code below, then work through the questions.** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(ncdf4) cmip_dir <- file.path(data_dir, "cmip6", "monthly-averaged") cmip_files <- list( ssp126_tas = "cmip6_regrided_model_ensemble.Thai_region.ssp126.tas.nc", ssp585_tas = "cmip6_regrided_model_ensemble.Thai_region.ssp585.tas.nc", ssp126_pr = "cmip6_regrided_model_ensemble.Thai_region.ssp126.pr.nc", ssp585_pr = "cmip6_regrided_model_ensemble.Thai_region.ssp585.pr.nc" ) # Read one CMIP variable as a list of per-model SpatRasters (time layers), # returning the models, times, and the raster grid. Dimensions are read by # name so the code does not depend on storage order. read_cmip_var <- function(path, var, from_date = NULL) { nc <- nc_open(path) on.exit(nc_close(nc)) lon <- ncvar_get(nc, "lon"); lat <- ncvar_get(nc, "lat") tim <- ncvar_get(nc, "time") models <- ncvar_get(nc, "model") # time units -> dates tunits <- ncatt_get(nc, "time", "units")$value origin <- as.Date(sub(".*since ", "", tunits)) dates <- floor_date(origin + tim, "month") arr <- ncvar_get(nc, var) # dims in file order dn <- sapply(nc$var[[var]]$dim, function(d) d$name) # reorder to lon, lat, time, model perm <- match(c("lon", "lat", "time", "model"), dn) arr <- aperm(arr, perm) # Trim to the reanalysis start onwards (as in the original workflow), so the # historical fit and the future projection share the same start month. if (!is.null(from_date)) { keep <- dates >= from_date arr <- arr[, , keep, , drop = FALSE] dates <- dates[keep] } list(arr = arr, lon = lon, lat = lat, dates = dates, models = models) } cmip_start <- min(clim_months) # reanalysis start month tas126 <- read_cmip_var(file.path(cmip_dir, cmip_files$ssp126_tas), "tas", cmip_start) cat("CMIP6 grid:", length(tas126$lon), "lon x", length(tas126$lat), "lat;", length(tas126$models), "models;", length(tas126$dates), "months\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` CMIP6 grid: 12 lon x 19 lat; 45 models; 912 months ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Build a raster template on the reanalysis grid so CMIP can be interpolated # onto it (matching the original workflow), then averaged per province. ref_grid <- tas_r[[1]] # reanalysis grid (already cropped to region) # Interpolate a per-model, per-time CMIP array onto the reanalysis grid and # average over each province. Returns a tidy data frame of provincial means. # Build a north-up, multi-layer SpatRaster from one model's [lon, lat, time] # slice. Handles either coordinate ordering and treats the lon/lat values as # cell centres (so the extent is padded by half a cell each side). slice_to_rast <- function(slc, lon, lat, ref) { lon_o <- order(lon); lat_o <- order(lat) # index ascending slc <- slc[lon_o, lat_o, , drop = FALSE] # lon asc, lat asc a <- aperm(slc, c(2, 1, 3)) # -> lat x lon x time a <- a[dim(a)[1]:1, , , drop = FALSE] # flip: north (max lat) = row 1 r <- rast(a) # 3rd dim -> layers lo <- sort(lon); la <- sort(lat) dx <- mean(diff(lo)); dy <- mean(diff(la)) ext(r) <- c(min(lo) - dx/2, max(lo) + dx/2, min(la) - dy/2, max(la) + dy/2) crs(r) <- crs(ref) # match the reanalysis grid r } # Interpolate every model's CMIP slice onto the reanalysis grid and average per # province, KEEPING the per-model detail (province x month x model). We keep the # per-model array because the Part 6 projection needs the model spread, not just # the ensemble mean. cmip_provincial_bymodel <- function(cv, var, ref, provinces) { nm <- length(cv$models) # Optional speed/debug knob: set env var CMIP_MODEL_CAP to a small integer to # use only the first few models (much faster). Unset / 0 uses all models. cap <- suppressWarnings(as.integer(Sys.getenv("CMIP_MODEL_CAP", ""))) if (!is.na(cap) && cap > 0) nm <- min(nm, cap) out <- vector("list", nm) for (m in seq_len(nm)) { slc <- cv$arr[, , , m] # lon x lat x time r <- slice_to_rast(slc, cv$lon, cv$lat, ref) names(r) <- as.character(cv$dates) if (var == "pr") r <- r * 86400 / 1000 # flux (kg m-2 s-1) -> m (as in original) else r <- r - 273.15 # Kelvin -> Celsius r <- resample(r, ref, method = "bilinear") # interpolate onto reanalysis grid pm <- region_means(r) # strict cell-centre assignment (as Part 3) out[[m]] <- pm[provinces, , drop = FALSE] } arr3 <- simplify2array(out) # province x month x model list(arr = arr3, dates = cv$dates, provinces = provinces) } # Ensemble summary (mean + interquartile band) from a per-model array. summarise_cmip <- function(bm, scenario, var) { arr3 <- bm$arr; nt <- length(bm$dates) qf <- function(p) as.vector(apply(arr3, c(1, 2), quantile, probs = p, na.rm = TRUE)) data.frame( province_en = rep(bm$provinces, times = nt), month = rep(bm$dates, each = length(bm$provinces)), scenario = scenario, variable = var, mean = as.vector(apply(arr3, c(1, 2), mean, na.rm = TRUE)), q25 = qf(0.25), q75 = qf(0.75) ) } ``` ::: ::: {.todo-note} The CMIP6 interpolation-and-averaging above is the single heaviest step and the part most likely to need a check on first render (it reads a 45-model ensemble and resamples it onto the reanalysis grid, exactly as the original Python did). If it is too slow for a live session, the course team may wish to pre-compute the provincial summaries once and cache them. It is left as a direct port here so the method is transparent. ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} tas585 <- read_cmip_var(file.path(cmip_dir, cmip_files$ssp585_tas), "tas", cmip_start) pr126 <- read_cmip_var(file.path(cmip_dir, cmip_files$ssp126_pr), "pr", cmip_start) pr585 <- read_cmip_var(file.path(cmip_dir, cmip_files$ssp585_pr), "pr", cmip_start) # Restrict all four files to the CLIMATE MODELS they have in common, in the same # order (as the original workflow does). The files hold different numbers of # models, and the projection pairs temperature and rainfall model-by-model, so # they must refer to the same models in the same order. common_models <- sort(Reduce(intersect, list(tas126$models, tas585$models, pr126$models, pr585$models))) align_models <- function(cv) { idx <- match(common_models, cv$models) cv$arr <- cv$arr[, , , idx, drop = FALSE] cv$models <- common_models cv } tas126 <- align_models(tas126); tas585 <- align_models(tas585) pr126 <- align_models(pr126); pr585 <- align_models(pr585) cat("CMIP models in common across all four files:", length(common_models), "\n") # Per-model provincial climate (kept for the Part 6 projection) cmip_tas <- list( "SSP1-2.6" = cmip_provincial_bymodel(tas126, "tas", ref_grid, selected_provinces), "SSP5-8.5" = cmip_provincial_bymodel(tas585, "tas", ref_grid, selected_provinces)) cmip_pr <- list( "SSP1-2.6" = cmip_provincial_bymodel(pr126, "pr", ref_grid, selected_provinces), "SSP5-8.5" = cmip_provincial_bymodel(pr585, "pr", ref_grid, selected_provinces)) # Ensemble summary for plotting cmip_summary <- bind_rows( summarise_cmip(cmip_tas[["SSP1-2.6"]], "SSP1-2.6", "tas"), summarise_cmip(cmip_tas[["SSP5-8.5"]], "SSP5-8.5", "tas"), summarise_cmip(cmip_pr[["SSP1-2.6"]], "SSP1-2.6", "pr"), summarise_cmip(cmip_pr[["SSP5-8.5"]], "SSP5-8.5", "pr") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` CMIP models in common across all four files: 41 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Annual means for a clearer long-range view, for one example province ----- example_province <- selected_provinces[1] annual <- cmip_summary %>% mutate(year = year(month)) %>% filter(province_en == example_province) %>% group_by(province_en, scenario, variable, year) %>% summarise(mean = mean(mean), q25 = mean(q25), q75 = mean(q75), .groups = "drop") lab <- c(tas = "temperature (°C)", pr = "total precipitation (m)") print(ggplot(annual, aes(year, mean, colour = scenario, fill = scenario)) + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25, ymax = q75), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", labeller = labeller(variable = lab)) + scale_colour_manual(values = c("SSP1-2.6" = "#2166ac", "SSP5-8.5" = "#b2182b")) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("SSP1-2.6" = "#2166ac", "SSP5-8.5" = "#b2182b")) + labs(x = "year", y = NULL, title = paste0("CMIP6 projections for ", example_province), subtitle = "Model mean (line) and interquartile range (band)") + theme_bw()) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part4-plot-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: **4.1 The projections are shown as a model mean with an interquartile band. Why do we show a *range* across models rather than picking the single "best" model?** ```{=html} ```


**4.2 Compare the two scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5). How far apart are the temperature projections by the end of the century? What about rainfall: is the model agreement as strong?** ```{=html} ```


**4.3 We are about to feed these projections into a malaria model that was fitted on only a couple of years of reanalysis. What assumption are we making about the climate–malaria relationship when we extrapolate it decades into the future?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 4: click to reveal (try the questions first) **4.1** Individual climate models disagree, especially on rainfall. The ensemble mean is more reliable than any single model, and the spread (here the interquartile band) is itself the honest expression of projection *uncertainty*. Picking one "best" model hides that uncertainty. **4.2** Temperature projections usually diverge clearly between the two scenarios by late century, with SSP5-8.5 substantially warmer, and models tend to agree on the direction of warming. Rainfall is far less certain: the inter-model band is usually wide and the scenarios may overlap, reflecting genuine disagreement about how precipitation will change. **4.3** We are assuming the climate–malaria relationship learned over a couple of recent years holds, unchanged, decades into the future, i.e. that no adaptation, intervention, land-use change, drug resistance, or shift in the mosquito/parasite system alters it. That is a strong assumption and the main caveat on any such projection. ::: --- # **Part 5:** Fitting climate to incidence ::: speaker-note This is the core of the session: a statistical model relating climate to malaria cases across all provinces at once (a "panel" model). The design choices to draw out: (1) we model the *first difference* (month-to-month change) in cases rather than the level, and we *standardise* per province, so that provinces of very different sizes contribute comparably; (2) climate enters as temperature, rainfall, and their squares, allowing a hump-shaped (optimum) response; and (3) the fitted coefficients are *shared* across provinces, one climate–malaria relationship applied everywhere. This is a direct translation of the original "panel method" ordinary-least-squares fit. Frame it explicitly as the *simplest* model, one set of coefficients for the whole country. Part 6 then pushes it forward under CMIP6 to project future cases; the optional SEIRS section at the end of this session is the more scientifically sound alternative (per-province transmission) for those who want a mechanistic model. ::: We now link climate to cases directly with a simple statistical model, pooling all provinces. We use a **panel method**, the same approach used to learn the impact of temperature and rainfall on the gross domestic product of the world's economies ([Burke, Hsiang & Miguel, *Nature*, 2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature15725){target="_blank"}) , but here applied to learn the impact of temperature and rainfall on the month-to-month *change* in infection count. Because these simple panel methods cannot account for trends, we first remove the trend by taking the first difference, then standardise each province by its own mean and standard deviation over time. Temperature and rainfall enter as themselves and squared, allowing a non-linear (hump-shaped) response, with one shared relationship across provinces. Because we model the first difference (the "integrated" step) and reconstruct the level by a cumulative sum, this is an **ARIMA-style** model, more precisely an autoregressive model with exogenous inputs (ARX), with temperature and rainfall as the exogenous climate drivers (hence `arx_fit` below). This is perhaps the most basic model one can build, and is designed to illustrate the logic of integrating the climate data. It uses a single set of coefficients for all provinces. In Part 6 we push this fitted model forward under the CMIP6 climate to project future cases. The optional SEIRS section at the end of this session is more scientifically sound (it has per-province transmission) and gives you a code base for fitting a transmission model directly. **Run the code below, then work through the questions.** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Align cases and climate on the same province x month grid ---------------- provinces <- selected_provinces months <- sort(unique(clim_long$month)) # Case matrix: provinces x months, zero-filled where no cases were reported cases_mat <- counts_prov_month %>% filter(province_en %in% provinces) %>% complete(province_en = provinces, month = months, fill = list(Count = 0)) %>% filter(month %in% months) %>% arrange(province_en, month) %>% pivot_wider(names_from = month, values_from = Count) %>% tibble::column_to_rownames("province_en") %>% as.matrix() cases_mat <- cases_mat[provinces, , drop = FALSE] # Climate matrices on the same grid mat_from_long <- function(var) { clim_long %>% select(province_en, month, all_of(var)) %>% pivot_wider(names_from = month, values_from = all_of(var)) %>% tibble::column_to_rownames("province_en") %>% as.matrix() %>% `[`(provinces, , drop = FALSE) } clim_temp <- mat_from_long("temp_k") # Kelvin (offset irrelevant once standardised) clim_rain <- mat_from_long("rain_m") cat("cases:", nrow(cases_mat), "provinces x", ncol(cases_mat), "months;", "total cases =", sum(cases_mat), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` cases: 15 provinces x 15 months; total cases = 12829 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Standardise per province ------------------------------------------------ # Use the population standard deviation (divide by n, not n-1) to match NumPy's # np.std in the original workflow. R's built-in sd() uses n-1, which would shift # the standardisation and change the fitted coefficients. popsd <- function(x) sqrt(mean((x - mean(x))^2)) # Response: standardised first difference of cases, per province cases_fd <- cases_mat[, -1] - cases_mat[, -ncol(cases_mat)] fd_mean <- rowMeans(cases_fd) fd_sd <- apply(cases_fd, 1, popsd) z_cases_fd <- (cases_fd - fd_mean) / fd_sd # Predictors: standardised temperature and rainfall, per province z_temp <- (clim_temp - rowMeans(clim_temp)) / apply(clim_temp, 1, popsd) z_rain <- (clim_rain - rowMeans(clim_rain)) / apply(clim_rain, 1, popsd) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Ordinary least squares on the pooled (flattened) panel ------------------ # Predictors are temperature, temperature^2, rainfall, rainfall^2, using the # months aligned with the first differences (drop the first month). Y <- as.vector(z_cases_fd) zt <- z_temp[, -1]; zr <- z_rain[, -1] X <- cbind(as.vector(zt), as.vector(zt)^2, as.vector(zr), as.vector(zr)^2) # Centre (as in the original), solve, and predict Y_mean <- mean(Y); Y_prime <- Y - Y_mean X_mean <- colMeans(X); X_prime <- sweep(X, 2, X_mean) beta <- solve(t(X_prime) %*% X_prime, t(X_prime) %*% Y_prime) Y_hat <- as.vector(X_prime %*% beta) + Y_mean colnames(X) <- c("temp", "temp^2", "rain", "rain^2") print(round(setNames(as.vector(beta), colnames(X)), 3)) # Reshape prediction back to province x month, and rebuild case counts by # reversing the standardisation and the first-differencing (cumulative sum). z_hat <- matrix(Y_hat, nrow = nrow(z_cases_fd)) fitted_cases <- cases_mat * 0 for (i in seq_len(nrow(cases_mat))) { fitted_cases[i, 1] <- cases_mat[i, 1] fitted_cases[i, -1] <- fitted_cases[i, 1] + cumsum(z_hat[i, ] * fd_sd[i] + fd_mean[i]) } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` temp temp^2 rain rain^2 0.241 0.295 0.096 -0.076 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Observed vs fitted monthly cases, per province -------------------------- fit_long <- bind_rows( as.data.frame(cases_mat) %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("province_en") %>% pivot_longer(-province_en, names_to = "month", values_to = "cases") %>% mutate(series = "observed"), as.data.frame(fitted_cases) %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("province_en") %>% pivot_longer(-province_en, names_to = "month", values_to = "cases") %>% mutate(series = "fitted") ) %>% mutate(month = as.Date(month)) print(ggplot(fit_long, aes(month, cases, colour = series)) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~ province_en, scales = "free_y") + scale_colour_manual(values = c(observed = "black", fitted = "red")) + labs(x = "date", y = "monthly cases", title = "Observed vs panel-fitted monthly cases") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part5-diag-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: The fitted `beta` coefficients define the climate response. In **Part 6** we apply them to the future CMIP6 climate to project cases forward. **5.1 The model uses temperature, temperature², rainfall, and rainfall². What shape of climate–malaria relationship do the squared terms allow that a straight-line model could not? Why is that biologically sensible for a mosquito-borne disease?** ```{=html} ```


**5.2 We standardised cases and climate *per province* before pooling. What would go wrong if we pooled the raw counts across provinces without standardising?** ```{=html} ```


**5.3 Look at the observed-vs-fitted panels. Where does the model do well, and where does it do poorly? What might it be missing (think about what drives malaria besides climate)?** ```{=html} ```


**5.4 To project future cases, we would apply these fitted coefficients to the CMIP6 climate from Part 4. List one thing you would want to check about the future climate values before trusting such a projection.** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 5: click to reveal (try the questions first) **5.1** The squared terms allow a *hump-shaped* (unimodal) response: cases rising with temperature or rainfall up to an optimum and then falling. This is biologically sensible because mosquito survival, biting, and parasite development each have thermal optima, and both drought and flushing rains can reduce breeding, so the response to climate is rarely monotonic. **5.2** Provinces differ enormously in population and case numbers. Pooling raw counts would let a few large-caseload provinces dominate the fit, and would confound "more cases because bigger" with "more cases because of climate". Standardising per province puts every province on a comparable footing so the shared coefficients reflect the climate relationship, not province size. **5.3** The model tends to capture the broad seasonal rise and fall but misses sharp peaks, sudden outbreaks, and province-specific idiosyncrasies. Malaria is also driven by non-climate factors: interventions (bed nets, spraying), human movement and importation, drug resistance, health-system reporting changes, none of which are in a climate-only model. **5.4** Sensible checks include: are the future climate values within the range the model was trained on (extrapolation beyond the training range is dangerous)? Are the units and standardisation applied identically to the projections? Is the seasonal structure of the projections realistic? Does the province averaging match? ::: --- # **Part 6:** Projecting future cases (statistical model) ::: speaker-note This is the pay-off: we take the model fitted on the historical period (Part 5) and push it forward under the CMIP6 climate (Part 4) to project future cases. The mechanics: standardise each future climate value using the *historical* per-province mean and standard deviation (never re-standardise on the future data, or you throw away the trend you are trying to project), apply the fitted `beta`, and rebuild case counts. We run every climate model separately and show the ensemble mean and interquartile band, exactly as we did for the raw climate in Part 4. Be sure to make the key caveat explicit (see the warning box): reconstructing counts by cumulatively summing predicted month-to-month changes over ~75 years behaves like a random walk and will drift. This is a genuine limitation of projecting a differenced/ARX model far ahead, and a large part of *why* one might prefer a bounded mechanistic model (the optional SEIRS section at the end of this session) for long horizons. Treat the projection as illustrative of method, not as a forecast. ::: We now push the fitted model forward. For each climate model and scenario, we standardise the future CMIP6 temperature and rainfall using the **historical** per-province mean and standard deviation (the same scaling the model was fitted with), apply the fitted coefficients to get the predicted month-to-month change in cases, and rebuild counts by cumulative sum, seeding from the last observed month. ::: {.callout-warning} ## A projection, not a forecast Rebuilding case counts by cumulatively summing predicted monthly *changes* over decades behaves like a random walk and will drift, so the absolute numbers far into the future should not be read literally. This is an inherent limitation of pushing a first-difference (ARX) model a long way forward, and is one reason a bounded mechanistic model (the optional SEIRS section at the end of this session) is often preferred for long-range projection. Use this to see *how* climate projections feed a fitted model, not as a prediction of future case counts. ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Historical per-province climate scaling, in the SAME units as the CMIP arrays # (temperature in °C, rainfall in m). Standardising is unaffected by a constant # offset, so these reproduce the scaling used to fit the model in Part 5. temp_mean_p <- rowMeans(clim_temp) - 273.15 # clim_temp is Kelvin; CMIP tas is °C temp_sd_p <- apply(clim_temp, 1, popsd) rain_mean_p <- rowMeans(clim_rain) rain_sd_p <- apply(clim_rain, 1, popsd) last_obs <- cases_mat[, ncol(cases_mat)] # seed the projection at the last observed month hist_end <- max(months) # Apply the fitted ARX model to one climate model's future climate, rebuild counts. project_one <- function(tas_pm, pr_pm, dates) { zt <- (tas_pm - temp_mean_p) / temp_sd_p # province x month (vectors recycle per row) zr <- (pr_pm - rain_mean_p) / rain_sd_p yhat <- (zt - X_mean[1]) * beta[1] + # predicted standardised first-difference (zt^2 - X_mean[2]) * beta[2] + (zr - X_mean[3]) * beta[3] + (zr^2 - X_mean[4]) * beta[4] + Y_mean dcases <- yhat * fd_sd + fd_mean # back to case-change units, per province fut <- which(dates > hist_end) # project future months only proj <- last_obs + t(apply(dcases[, fut, drop = FALSE], 1, cumsum)) list(proj = proj, dates = dates[fut]) } # scenario -> array (province x future-month x model), dropping unstable models project_scenario <- function(scenario) { tas <- cmip_tas[[scenario]]; pr <- cmip_pr[[scenario]] nm <- dim(tas$arr)[3] lst <- lapply(seq_len(nm), function(m) project_one(tas$arr[, , m], pr$arr[, , m], tas$dates)$proj) arr <- simplify2array(lst) # province x month x model keep <- apply(arr, 3, function(x) all(is.finite(x))) arr[, , keep, drop = FALSE] } fut_dates <- project_one(cmip_tas[[1]]$arr[, , 1], cmip_pr[[1]]$arr[, , 1], cmip_tas[[1]]$dates)$dates proj_arr <- list("SSP1-2.6" = project_scenario("SSP1-2.6"), "SSP5-8.5" = project_scenario("SSP5-8.5")) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Annual totals per province, with ensemble mean and interquartile band -------- annual_proj <- function(arr, dates, scenario) { yr <- lubridate::year(dates); years <- sort(unique(yr)) ap <- sapply(years, function(y) apply(arr[, yr == y, , drop = FALSE], c(1, 3), sum)) # -> (province*?)... ap <- array(ap, dim = c(length(provinces), dim(arr)[3], length(years))) # prov x model x year ap <- aperm(ap, c(1, 3, 2)) # prov x year x model data.frame( province_en = rep(provinces, times = length(years)), year = rep(years, each = length(provinces)), scenario = scenario, mean = as.vector(apply(ap, c(1, 2), mean)), q25 = as.vector(apply(ap, c(1, 2), quantile, 0.25)), q75 = as.vector(apply(ap, c(1, 2), quantile, 0.75)) ) } proj_annual <- bind_rows( annual_proj(proj_arr[["SSP1-2.6"]], fut_dates, "SSP1-2.6"), annual_proj(proj_arr[["SSP5-8.5"]], fut_dates, "SSP5-8.5") ) print(ggplot(proj_annual, aes(year, mean, colour = scenario, fill = scenario)) + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25, ymax = q75), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~ province_en, scales = "free_y") + scale_colour_manual(values = c("SSP1-2.6" = "#2166ac", "SSP5-8.5" = "#b2182b")) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("SSP1-2.6" = "#2166ac", "SSP5-8.5" = "#b2182b")) + labs(x = "year", y = "projected annual cases", title = "Projected annual cases under CMIP6 (statistical/ARX model)", subtitle = "Ensemble mean (line) and interquartile range (band). See caveat above.") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/part6-plot-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: **6.1 The projection standardises the future climate using the *historical* mean and standard deviation, not the future data's own. Why is that essential? What would go wrong if we standardised the future climate on itself?** ```{=html} ```


**6.2 Look at the projected trajectories and re-read the caveat. Which features of these plots would you trust, and which would you not? How would you describe the result honestly to a decision-maker?** ```{=html} ```


::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Solutions for Part 6: click to reveal (try the questions first) **6.1** The model was fitted on climate standardised by its *historical* mean and standard deviation, so the coefficients only make sense on that same scale. If we re-standardised the future climate on itself, we would subtract off the very warming (or drying/wetting) trend we are trying to project; every period would look "average" relative to its own era, and the projected climate signal would vanish. We must apply the historical scaling so that a genuinely hotter future shows up as a large positive standardised value. **6.2** Trust the *direction and relative* differences, e.g. that the higher- emissions scenario diverges from the lower one, and the ranking of provinces, more than the absolute case numbers, which drift because of the cumulative-sum reconstruction. Honest framing to a decision-maker: "this shows how a climate-driven statistical model *responds* to two emissions futures; the shape and the gap between scenarios are informative, but the absolute counts decades out are illustrative, not a forecast, and a mechanistic model would be needed for credible long-range numbers." ::: --- # Optional: a mechanistic SEIRS alternative ::: speaker-note This whole section is optional and more advanced: it lives behind an expandable box so it does not interrupt the main flow, and so Session 3 can stay a clean group discussion. Run it only if the group powers through Parts 1–6 with time to spare, or point interested participants to it afterwards. It reuses the case and climate data already built above (no re-loading), fits a compartmental transmission model, and doubles as a **worked code base for fitting a transmission model directly**. Keep the optimiser budget small for a live demo: the fit is deliberately not run to convergence, and every number is illustrative. ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Why an SEIRS model? (optional and more advanced; click to expand) The model in Parts 5–6 is deliberately the simplest thing that works: one shared statistical relationship between climate and the month-to-month *change* in cases. A mechanistic **SEIRS** model is the more biologically structured alternative. Instead of regressing cases on climate, it represents the disease process itself: people move through Susceptible → Exposed → Infected → Recovered → Susceptible compartments , and lets **climate drive the transmission rate**. That buys three things the statistical model cannot: per-province transmission with *shared* natural-history parameters (so provinces borrow strength); built-in saturation and waning immunity (so it cannot produce the negative or runaway counts a differenced model can); and a template you can adapt to fit a transmission model directly to your own data. The cost is more assumptions and a harder fit. Each province runs an SEIRS model sharing the natural-history parameters but with a province- and time-varying transmission rate: $$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta(t) S I + \omega R \quad \frac{dE}{dt} = \beta(t) S I - \sigma E \quad \frac{dI}{dt} = \sigma E - \gamma I \quad \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I - \omega R$$ with $\beta_p(t) = \beta_{0,p}\exp(b_{temp}\, z_{temp}(p,t) + b_{rain}\, z_{rain}(p,t))$, and reported cases modelled as $\text{cases}_p(t) \sim \text{Poisson}(\text{scale}_p \cdot \sigma E_p(t))$, fitted by maximum likelihood. **Caveats: read before trusting any number.** The climate covariates stand in for missing mosquito data; there are no population denominators (so `scale` absorbs population size and reporting rate); the natural-history parameters ($\sigma,\gamma,\omega$) are fitted freely rather than fixed to published vivax/falciparum biology; the two parasite species are pooled; and the optimiser uses a small iteration budget so it runs quickly; it is **not** run to convergence. Treat every output as illustrative of method, not as an estimate. **The data.** The SEIRS reuses the case matrix and per-province climate already built in Parts 2–3 and 5. The only difference is that it standardises climate **globally** (one shared scale) rather than per province, so that `b_temp`/`b_rain` are a single comparable climate sensitivity. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # reuse cases_mat, clim_temp (Kelvin), clim_rain (m), popsd() from Part 5 ztemp <- (clim_temp - mean(clim_temp)) / popsd(clim_temp) zrain <- (clim_rain - mean(clim_rain)) / popsd(clim_rain) cat(sprintf("SEIRS inputs: %d provinces x %d months, %.0f total cases\n", nrow(cases_mat), ncol(cases_mat), sum(cases_mat))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` SEIRS inputs: 15 provinces x 15 months, 12829 total cases ``` ::: ::: The model is built up in five small pieces below, then fitted. Read them in order. **Step 1: the transmission dynamics (the ODE right-hand side).** This is the SEIRS model itself: the instantaneous rate of change of each compartment. `state` is a 5 × n matrix, one column per province, rows S, E, I, R, and C (a running count of new infections), so everything is vectorised over provinces at once. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} SUBSTEPS_PER_MONTH <- 20 # RK4 sub-steps per month seirs_rhs <- function(state, beta, sigma, gamma, omega) { S <- state[1, ]; E <- state[2, ]; I <- state[3, ]; R <- state[4, ] rbind(-beta * S * I + omega * R, # dS (lose to infection, gain from waning immunity) beta * S * I - sigma * E, # dE (newly infected, then become infectious) sigma * E - gamma * I, # dI (become infectious, then recover) gamma * I - omega * R, # dR (recover, then lose immunity) sigma * E) # dC (cumulative NEW infections) } ``` ::: **Step 2: simulating it forward (fixed-step RK4).** We integrate the ODE month by month using a fixed-step Runge–Kutta (RK4) scheme with `SUBSTEPS_PER_MONTH` steps per month, for all provinces simultaneously. A burn-in lets the model settle before we record anything, and we reset the accumulator `C` at the start of each month so that `incidence` holds the new infections *within* that month. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} simulate_incidence <- function(beta, sigma, gamma, omega, i0 = 1e-4, e0 = 1e-4, burn_in_months = 12) { n_prov <- nrow(beta); n_months <- ncol(beta); dt <- 1 / SUBSTEPS_PER_MONTH state <- rbind(rep(1 - i0 - e0, n_prov), rep(e0, n_prov), rep(i0, n_prov), rep(0, n_prov), rep(0, n_prov)) step <- function(state, beta_m) { # one RK4 sub-step k1 <- seirs_rhs(state, beta_m, sigma, gamma, omega) k2 <- seirs_rhs(state + 0.5*dt*k1, beta_m, sigma, gamma, omega) k3 <- seirs_rhs(state + 0.5*dt*k2, beta_m, sigma, gamma, omega) k4 <- seirs_rhs(state + dt*k3, beta_m, sigma, gamma, omega) state + (dt/6) * (k1 + 2*k2 + 2*k3 + k4) } if (burn_in_months > 0) { # settle using month-1 transmission beta0_m <- beta[, 1] for (b in seq_len(burn_in_months)) { for (s in seq_len(SUBSTEPS_PER_MONTH)) state <- step(state, beta0_m) state[5, ] <- 0 } } incidence <- matrix(0, n_prov, n_months) for (m in seq_len(n_months)) { state[5, ] <- 0; beta_m <- beta[, m] # reset accumulator; use this month's beta for (s in seq_len(SUBSTEPS_PER_MONTH)) state <- step(state, beta_m) incidence[, m] <- state[5, ] state[1:4, ] <- pmin(pmax(state[1:4, ], 0), 1) # keep compartments in [0, 1] } incidence } ``` ::: **Step 3: unpacking the parameters.** The optimiser works in an unconstrained, log-transformed space so that rates stay positive. This turns the flat parameter vector back into named, natural-scale quantities: the shared natural-history rates, the two climate sensitivities, and the per-province baseline transmission `beta0` and reporting `scale`. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} unpack_params <- function(params, n) { list(sigma = exp(params[1]), gamma = exp(params[2]), omega = exp(params[3]), b_temp = params[4], b_rain = params[5], beta0 = exp(params[6:(5 + n)]), scale = exp(params[(6 + n):(5 + 2*n)])) } ``` ::: **Step 4: the likelihood.** Given a parameter vector, build the climate-forced transmission rate `beta_t`, simulate the incidence, and score it against the observed counts with a Poisson negative log-likelihood, the quantity the optimiser minimises. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} neg_log_lik <- function(params, cases, ztemp, zrain) { n <- nrow(cases); p <- unpack_params(params, n) beta_t <- p$beta0 * exp(p$b_temp * ztemp + p$b_rain * zrain) # climate forces transmission beta_t <- pmin(pmax(beta_t, 1e-6), 50) incidence <- simulate_incidence(beta_t, p$sigma, p$gamma, p$omega) lam <- pmax(p$scale * incidence, 1e-8) # expected reported cases sum(lam - cases * log(lam)) # Poisson NLL (constant omitted) } ``` ::: **Step 5: sensible starting values.** A reasonable initial guess for the optimiser: incubation ~0.5 months, infectious ~1 month, immunity ~6 months, no climate effect to begin with, and per-province baselines scaled to each province's mean caseload. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} default_x0 <- function(cases) { n <- nrow(cases) c(log(1/0.5), log(1/1.0), log(1/6.0), 0, 0, # sigma, gamma, omega, b_temp, b_rain log(rep(2.5, n)), # beta0 (per province) log(pmax(rowMeans(cases), 1) * 50)) # scale (per province) } ``` ::: **Step 6: fit.** Now minimise the negative log-likelihood with a bounded optimiser (`L-BFGS-B`). The `lower`/`upper` vectors keep every parameter in a biologically plausible range (incubation/infectious periods between ~0.05 and 3 months, immunity between 0.5 and 36 months, the climate sensitivities within ±2), and `maxit` is kept deliberately small so it finishes quickly (raise it for a real fit). ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} n <- nrow(cases_mat) x0 <- default_x0(cases_mat) lower <- c(log(1/3.0), log(1/3.0), log(1/36.0), -2, -2, rep(log(0.05), n), rep(log(1.0), n)) upper <- c(log(1/0.05), log(1/0.05), log(1/0.5), 2, 2, rep(log(30), n), rep(log(1e9), n)) # maxit kept small so this runs quickly; raise it for a production fit. ndeps sets # the finite-difference gradient step to match SciPy's default, so this tracks the # notebook's fit as closely as the two L-BFGS-B implementations allow (see the note # in python_workflow/compare.R about why the fitted parameters are close, not exact). fit <- optim(x0, neg_log_lik, method = "L-BFGS-B", lower = lower, upper = upper, cases = cases_mat, ztemp = ztemp, zrain = zrain, control = list(maxit = 20, ndeps = rep(1e-8, length(x0)))) p <- unpack_params(fit$par, n) cat(sprintf("convergence code=%d nll=%.1f\n", fit$convergence, fit$value)) cat(sprintf("incubation period 1/sigma = %.2f months\n", 1/p$sigma)) cat(sprintf("infectious period 1/gamma = %.2f months\n", 1/p$gamma)) cat(sprintf("immunity duration 1/omega = %.2f months\n", 1/p$omega)) cat(sprintf("climate sensitivity b_temp = %+.3f b_rain = %+.3f\n", p$b_temp, p$b_rain)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` convergence code=1 nll=-52416.8 incubation period 1/sigma = 0.18 months infectious period 1/gamma = 0.86 months immunity duration 1/omega = 9.49 months climate sensitivity b_temp = +0.059 b_rain = +0.268 ``` ::: ::: **Step 7: diagnostics.** Finally, rebuild the fitted incidence from the estimated parameters and compare it, province by province, against the observed monthly counts. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} beta_fit <- p$beta0 * exp(p$b_temp * ztemp + p$b_rain * zrain) lam_fit <- p$scale * simulate_incidence(beta_fit, p$sigma, p$gamma, p$omega) ss_res <- sum((cases_mat - lam_fit)^2) ss_tot <- sum((cases_mat - mean(cases_mat))^2) cat(sprintf("pseudo-R2 (vs fitted lambda): %.3f\n", 1 - ss_res / ss_tot)) diag_long <- bind_rows( as.data.frame(cases_mat) %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("province_en") %>% pivot_longer(-province_en, names_to = "month", values_to = "cases") %>% mutate(series = "observed"), as.data.frame(lam_fit) %>% `rownames<-`(rownames(cases_mat)) %>% `colnames<-`(colnames(cases_mat)) %>% tibble::rownames_to_column("province_en") %>% pivot_longer(-province_en, names_to = "month", values_to = "cases") %>% mutate(series = "fitted") ) %>% mutate(month = as.Date(month)) print(ggplot(diag_long, aes(month, cases, colour = series)) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) + facet_wrap(~ province_en, scales = "free_y") + scale_colour_manual(values = c(observed = "black", fitted = "red")) + labs(x = "date", y = "monthly cases", title = "Observed vs SEIRS-fitted monthly cases") + theme_bw() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](session2_climate_data_files/figure-html/seirs-diag-1.png){fig-align='center' width=1056} ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` pseudo-R2 (vs fitted lambda): 0.944 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: **A note on identifiability.** Fitting this exact model in both R and Python is instructive. The two reach the *same* likelihood and predict essentially the same cases (per-province annual totals agree to about 0.3%), yet their fitted `beta0` and `scale` differ by a couple of percent. That is a textbook symptom of **non-identifiability**: `beta0` (baseline transmission) and `scale` (population × reporting rate) trade off to set the case *level*, so the optimiser can shuffle magnitude between them, and the natural-history rates ($\sigma,\gamma,\omega$) are only weakly constrained by the data. It is concrete evidence for the warning above: trust the model's *predictions* and the climate sensitivities `b_temp`/`b_rain`, not the individual parameter values. (This is why the verification in `python_workflow/compare.R` checks the fitted incidence and climate coefficients, and treats the level parameters as illustrative.) **Discussion (SEIRS): the statistical model (Parts 5–6) and this mechanistic model both link climate to malaria. What can the mechanistic model tell you that the statistical one cannot, and vice versa?
